title: Problem, target and success measure

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Problem, target and success measure; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An ML problem statement defines the prediction unit, target and observation time, decision user, action enabled, baseline, error costs, population, non-goals, and measurable success before model choice.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A support triage project predicts whether a ticket needs specialist review at intake, compares a rules baseline, and measures missed urgent cases plus reviewer workload. Your goal is to write a measurable ML problem statement with non-goals and baseline. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: python quality project; Problem and environment characteristics; pf problem io. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: An ML problem statement defines the prediction unit, target and observation time, decision user, action enabled, baseline, error costs, population, non-goals, and measurable success before model choice. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: A measurable label can still be a harmful proxy, historical decisions can contaminate targets, prediction may not improve the decision, metrics can conflict, and deployment prevalence can differ from training. The learner must reject this overclaim: Choosing an available target column and maximizing its test accuracy is enough to define a useful ML problem.

includes: Specify prediction unit, target, decision context and cost of errors before selecting a model.
Write a measurable ML problem statement with non-goals and baseline.
excludes: A measurable label can still be a harmful proxy, historical decisions can contaminate targets, prediction may not improve the decision, metrics can conflict, and deployment prevalence can differ from training.

outcomes: Write a measurable ML problem statement with non-goals and baseline.

examples.title: Problem, target and success measure: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A support triage project predicts whether a ticket needs specialist review at intake, compares a rules baseline, and measures missed urgent cases plus reviewer workload.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A support triage project predicts whether a ticket needs specialist review at intake, compares a rules baseline, and measures missed urgent cases plus reviewer workload.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Specify target timing, decision context, population, baseline, error costs, non-goals, and stop criteria; reject labels that do not represent the intended outcome.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Specify target timing, decision context, population, baseline, error costs, non-goals, and stop criteria; reject labels that do not represent the intended outcome.

examples.title: Problem, target and success measure: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Past approval is rejected as a creditworthiness target because it encodes prior policy and cannot establish the desired outcome fairly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Past approval is rejected as a creditworthiness target because it encodes prior policy and cannot establish the desired outcome fairly.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: A measurable label can still be a harmful proxy, historical decisions can contaminate targets, prediction may not improve the decision, metrics can conflict, and deployment prevalence can differ from training.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Choosing an available target column and maximizing its test accuracy is enough to define a useful ML problem.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write a measurable ML problem statement with non-goals and baseline.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Choosing an available target column and maximizing its test accuracy is enough to define a useful ML problem.
misconceptions.correction: Specify target timing, decision context, population, baseline, error costs, non-goals, and stop criteria; reject labels that do not represent the intended outcome.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Choosing an available target column and maximizing its test accuracy is enough to define a useful ML problem.

analogy.example: a provenance-controlled laboratory that defines the decision, inspects samples and labels, derives transformations, and seals evaluation partitions can introduce Problem, target and success measure only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: problem framing, data quality, preprocessing, and leakage control
limitations: clean procedures cannot recover absent populations, authorize data, or make a harmful target appropriate

recap.summary: You should now be able to write a measurable ML problem statement with non-goals and baseline. Keep this boundary in view: A measurable label can still be a harmful proxy, historical decisions can contaminate targets, prediction may not improve the decision, metrics can conflict, and deployment prevalence can differ from training.
